title: Boiler Power Calculator: kW per m² | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Boiler Size by Room Area & Insulation
description: Calculate boiler power needed by room size and insulation quality. Instant heating capacity sizing. Free heating system calculator.
intro: Heating system design requires precise calculations: boiler capacity, radiator sizing, heat loss factors, and efficiency optimization. Mistakes result in undersized systems (insufficient warmth) or oversized boilers (wasted energy and cost). This calculator determines boiler power (kW) based on your room area and insulation quality using industry-standard formulas. All values are current to 2026 and reviewed periodically to align with updated standards.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **kW ≈ m² × 0.08–0.10** depending on insulation. Direct application with your room size and insulation level. Results automatically suggest standard commercial boiler sizes (10, 20, 24, 28, 32 kW).
useCases: Technical calculations for boiler sizing and heating system design
Students and HVAC professionals planning heating capacity
Verify theoretical heating load calculations before purchasing
Educational reference for heating load concepts
Quick reference for residential and commercial heating projects
explanation:
**Heating system sizing** is a critical technical skill: boiler capacity, radiator placement, pipe sizing, and system efficiency. Industry standards like NIST and ASHRAE provide the formulas used here.

## How to Calculate

This calculator applies the boiler sizing formula using your inputs, checking results against realistic ranges and alerting if values fall outside normal parameters.

Insulation quality significantly affects the calculation:
- **Good insulation**: 0.07 kW/m²
- **Standard insulation**: 0.09 kW/m²
- **Poor insulation**: 0.12 kW/m²

Multiply your heated area by the appropriate factor to determine boiler capacity needed.

## Final Notes

This is a reference calculation for heating system design. For critical decisions (new construction, major renovations, energy upgrades), consult a licensed HVAC professional. Calculations are current to 2026 and reviewed periodically for accuracy.

faq.q: What size boiler do I need for my home?
faq.a: Enter your total heated area in m² and insulation quality. Formula: kW ≈ m² × 0.08–0.10. Standard residential boilers are sized 10, 20, 24, 28, or 32 kW—choose the next size up from your calculated result.

faq.q: How much does insulation affect boiler size?
faq.a: Significantly. Good insulation: 0.07 kW/m². Standard: 0.09 kW/m². Poor: 0.12 kW/m². Better insulation reduces heating requirements and allows smaller, more efficient boilers.

faq.q: What does boiler kW actually mean?
faq.a: Kilowatts (kW) measure heat output. One kW produces about 3,412 BTU per hour. Higher kW ratings handle larger spaces or colder climates. Match your boiler's kW to your calculated heating load.

faq.q: Does climate zone affect boiler sizing?
faq.a: Yes. Cold regions like northern climates need approximately 30% more capacity. Warm climates may use 20% less. Adjust your results based on your local heating season intensity.

faq.q: Radiators vs. radiant floor heating—which is better?
faq.a: Radiators require ~100 W per m². Radiant floor heating uses only 45–60 W/m² and is more efficient long-term. Floor heating costs more to install but saves significantly on operating costs and feels more comfortable.

faq.q: How accurate is this boiler calculator?
faq.a: Results use industry-standard formulas validated by NIST and international building codes. They're reliable for planning and estimation, but consult an HVAC professional for final engineering decisions.

faq.q: Is the boiler calculator really free?
faq.a: Yes, completely free. No registration required, no ads, no hidden fees. All our calculators are free resources.

faq.q: Do you store or share my calculations?
faq.a: No. All calculations run in your browser only. We never send your data to any server—your information stays completely private.

faq.q: When was this calculator last updated?
faq.a: The boiler sizing formula is based on physics constants and doesn't change. We review heating standards and assumptions annually to ensure continued accuracy.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Formula is a physics constant and does not require external data updates
